text: be what finally alerts them, and by then you won’t even be recognizable.” Time stopped. The air felt heavy and her ears rang with the pounding of her pulse. The weight finally lifted from her back and the pressure on her arm subsided. Heavy footsteps ran away, echoing the path of his escape. Erin began counting, eyes still closed, in an attempt to calm her heart and mind, to level out and pull herself together. Only when the cool evening air rushed up her pant leg did Erin feel the chill of embarrassment and realize she’d wet herself. Erin was found—facedown in a fetal position, still counting—by the third-floor lawyer who had parked next to her. The sound of the double-beep as he unlocked his car on approach made Erin break her silence and whimper, which got his attention. The rest of the evening was a blur of worried faces and information Erin wouldn’t have remembered without the discharge instructions. She vaguely recalled her pathetic description to the police. “Taller than me, dark hair, dark eyes, a really straight nose, and smelled of cheap whiskey.” Why did I think his straight nose was so important? Why did I even notice it? Erin had discussed both questions with her therapist, but still wondered about them. The police never caught him. She wasn’t surprised, but it did worry Erin, even though they had reassured her a second attack by the same perpetrator was as likely as being struck by lightning twice. Almost impossible. Erin turned to her left and considered the two cars against the exterior wall. It was the location she had been parked that fateful night, though on the fourth level. She paused and judged the distance to the elevator, honestly assessing the situation almost a year later. Erin shook her head. How had I been so unaware? Careful not to blame herself, Erin pushed away the questions she knew were dangerous—the questions that could bring on depression. No, when faced with the choice, Erin much preferred the strength she found tapping into the anger she wasn’t supposed to hold. Not a lot, not enough to land her back on medication, but just a scratch along the surface. Enough to remind Erin she had survived. But being in the garage alone was feeding the anger tiny morsels of rage, justified and pure. Erin was reliving the smell of whiskey and the foulness of the breath that had bore down on her. Her arm and shoulder throbbed at the memory of the pain, which earned her a sling for several weeks—the mental damage lasted so much longer. Erin recalled the embarrassment several hours later as police spoke to her at the Emergency Room, under lights bright enough to show off the wet spot where she’d pissed herself. For a moment she had believed the man was going to kill her—that she was truly going to die—and her fear had overridden her bladder control. Erin didn’t comprehend until much later, if she hadn’t made a noise, the lawyer may well have run her over, and Erin would have survived the mugging only to be murdered by her catatonia. Erin stared at the shadow between the two cars where her attacker had hidden and listened to her heartbeat in her ears. She didn’t hear the elevator ding behind her. She didn’t notice the door open. And when the man stepped forward, politely offering an evening salutation, Erin spun on instinct. Her tapped rage flooded Erin’s body, her eyes blurred, and her arm shot straight out. She would not be attacked again. She would not become a repeat victim. She would not cower below an aggressor or tremble in fear from empty threats. He would not win. The heel of her shoe made a wet sound as it punctured the man’s eye. The slick suctioning noise, which followed as Erin pulled the shoe back out, was muffled by her primal scream. Her eyes widened, her teeth bared, Erin rammed the shoes forward and drove the heel home a second time. As the man fell backwards, one shoe sticking out from his face, Erin’s hands opened and went to her mouth to cover her scream. The other shoe, and the keys she’d been holding, clattered to the ground. Erin peered down at the man. Her attacker. Adrenaline tasted sour in her mouth. Her heart pounded, a twin staccato to her breathing. Blood ran from his eyes, gravity drawing it down the side of his face to pool in his ear, and backward to soak into his blond hairline. Blond. Erin blinked and relived her final strike. Her arm had gone straight out in front of her. This man was her height, without shoes. Her mugger had been taller than she was in heels. He’d had dark hair, not blond. Erin noted the fallen man’s nose. “Oh no no no.” This was all wrong. This wasn’t her mugger. This wasn’t survival. This wasn’t revenge. This is murd— Erin wouldn’t allow herself to think the word. It wasn’t that. It was self-defense. He came out of the elevator— She paused and glanced at the doors as they slid shut. Or did I? She nodded slowly. Yes, that’s what happened. When I came out of the elevator, he was waiting for me. He ambushed me. She shook her head. No, that’s not right. Erin’s thoughts stopped making sense. Spinning to try and piece together reality, they had created a vortex of nothing and she swam in the silence of a blank slate for several minutes. But it could be what happened. She searched the parking garage again and saw it was still empty of people. She studied York Towers and squinted at the bank of dark office windows. Erin’s gaze returned to the body sprawled outside the elevator. She saw his pants were wet, and she registered the smell of urine in the night air. What have I done?
